q: Most of the fluids are not ideal ones and offer some resistance to motion.
Internal resistance to fluid motion is called friction analogous to viscosity when a solid move on a surface.
Viscosity measures resistance to flow arising due to the internal friction between the fluid layers as they slip past one another when fluid flows.
A fluid with low viscosity resists motion because its strong intermolecular forces give it a lot of internal friction.
The viscosity of liquids decreases rapidly with an increase in temperature.
The viscosity of gases decreases with an increase in temperature.
Viscosity is an intensive property because it does not change as the amount of matter changes.
The SI unit of viscosity is Newton second per metre ($N \cdot s \cdot m^{-1}$).
One way to measure fluid's viscosity is to measure resistance to flow when an external force is applied, which is known as dynamic viscosity.
Kinematic viscosity is less useful than dynamic (absolute) viscosity for measuring the resistive flow of a fluid under gravity.
Newton's law of viscosity states that the shear stress between the two adjacent layers of fluid is directly proportional to the negative value of the velocity gradient.
Shear stress is defined as the shearing force per unit volume.
Viscosity is defined as the ratio of shearing stress to the velocity gradient.
Stokes' law states that the drag force acting upward in resistance to the fall of a small spherical object in a viscous liquid is $F = 6 \pi \eta r^{2} v$.
Terminal velocity is the constant velocity of fall for small spherical objects through a fluid medium under the influence of gravity.
Drag force $F$ acts downward in resistance to the fall of a spherical object in a viscous liquid.
At a constant velocity of fall (terminal velocity), the upward and downward forces are in balance.
Dynamic viscosity is also referred to as absolute viscosity or kinematic viscosity interchangeably.
Gases exhibit viscosity, but it is harder to notice in ordinary circumstances compared to liquids.
Shear stress is represented by the symbol $\eta$ in Newton's law of viscosity.
